sentences.text: Well, I stayed there, I'm sure it was after New Year's because I heard the Germans were singing 'O Tannenbaum.' I remembered that because the sentries were singing, and they had a wreath.
Some sentries which were the guard places had wreaths.
I remember it was something to do with Christmas.
It was people.
They didn't have the religious books, but a lot of people knew everything from the-- they remember it.
And I heard people used to say, and it was a miracle, also something to tell us about-- they were writers.
People used to write, they used to take things, They used to write songs.
They used to write poems.
They used to inscribe on the walls so they could think.
Kept the holidays.
Some people kept the holidays.
Yeah.
They knew exactly.
They used to see a clock.
We had clocks.
We took a stick, put it in the ground and knew exactly the hour we live.
It was very unusual.
People were smart.
I must admit Jewish people are very, not just smart, they're very intelligent, have knowledge.
No.
They were lucky to have food.
I don't think so.
No.
You know why?
Number one, there was no such thing as kosher.
People needed food.
Food is the biggest object when you're hungry.
And the people, they went according to certain traditions, I know, like they couldn't fight nobody else.
They went their own ways.
They were suffering, I'm sure, but there were a lot of intelligent people in that group.
I cannot express to you.
They were the teachers.
There were some teachers I used to know in school.
There were some professors.
There were a few doctors there.
But somebody once told me like this, now it makes me say to you, I got to repeat that.
So the first thing it says the Germans were very smart.
They took out, they killed the smart people.
So anybody who was smart had to hide.
When they killed the smart people, they took out the strong people.
Why?
Strong people are the people who are rough, who were the [NON-ENGLISH].
A [NON-ENGLISH] is a person who carried boxes, you carried, was a shoemaker, was a man who was working steel, or make roofs.
Those were called the strong people.
Then they went after the communists because they were political.
But once they got through all these people, guess what, they came after me too.
I was the last guy on the list.
And I want to stress that because this was their philosophy how they killed the Jews.
Well, we arrived in next camp.
This camp supposedly supposed to be a [NON-ENGLISH] camp, a [NON-ENGLISH].
The German people were getting very frightened.
It was too many people dying, and they were trying to build up some people.
It was very close to the war ending.
They saw that the Germans-- the Americans were very close already.
You heard at night, I used to put my head to the bed and I used to hear the bullets, the cannons.
That's how I knew how near they were in Germany.
And this camp was a most unusual camp.
I just lay there.
There was no work.
It was an international camp.
There were American people in that camp in the same barrack of mine.
There was Russian people.
There were French people.
There were Italian people.
There was an Italian army called the Badoglio army.
Badoglio is the army that was against Mussolini.
It was in that camp.
And I remember exactly the hats they wore, like a little flap across their cap.
Then they had clergymen were there.
They had rabbis.
Nobody knew about it.
They had homosexual people and gypsies there in that particular camp, everything, and there was no more Jews, the Jewish camp.
Now, this camp was strictly an integrated camp.
Only men in that camp-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I've got to go back a moment.
I saw something.
One camp was-- Unterriexingen I saw-- but never mind.
In one camp, I don't know exactly, one camp I stayed on an appel one time, one side was men and one side was women.
I think it was Neckargerach, when we came to Neckargerach, this was.
On the other side was women.
It was very cold.
And in the snow was spots of blood because the woman were naked.
In fact, I put it in my book.
The wire was not electrified.
The only electric was this way, side all around, but the division was not electrified.
I don't think so.
Maybe it was.
And we looked over there.
They had an appel from the women and the spots of blood.
I'm sure they were menstruate or something else.
And this was, I remember as a child, as a young adult that's where I learned about women.
That's what I want to stress.
I'm sure they had menstruations.
I'm sure that a woman goes through a certain time period in her life.
No.
No.
Just staying naked and they didn't have the facilities, how to clean them, so just drip down their legs.
Oh, yeah.
I was a naked [NON-ENGLISH] when I was there.
They took all our clothes because our clothes were completely infected with lice.
And I kept my hat, my cap.
And they gave me a huge blanket, they gave me.
And I covered myself.
I lay there.
I can't tell you exactly, but I remember it was very cold there.
It was already January or February, 1945.
But I laid in that bed and I thought my father was never there, which I never met him.
I was already that's it.
That's the place that I will be buried.
And I only knew my cousin, that he's alive, and I'm sure he's an old man right now.
He laid there too.
That same bank, these banks were a straight piece of wood, and the front was like a little-- to hold you back from sliding off.
There was straw.
No mattresses, straw.
And we just laid there, and all different nationalities were there.
But these people were different than the Jews.
They had more-- how would you say the word?
They had more like more power, the Polish people, Russian people, everybody.
Everybody wore a triangle.
This triangle signified the crime they committed.
We had a yellow triangle right here with a J in the middle.
They had a red one for one crime.
They had a blue one, a purple one, a green one.
Any kind of crime they committed was a crime right here.
On the top was a number and the bottom, about 2 inches from the bottom, was a triangle, large triangle right here.
Yeah.
No.
No, the only place that had tattoo was in Auschwitz and I was in these camps.
Nobody was tattooed in these camps.
We were lying there practically naked, covered in a blanket.
And there was a lady-- no, no.
There was a guy came by.
They had a cow.
They killed a cow in the kitchen.
It was a different kind of camp.
This was a different kind of camp than the other ones was.
This was more camp.
There was more eating.
It was a [NON-ENGLISH].
They want to build you up a bit, but I was so gone with the ground, I must have weighed less than I weighed before.
I was so skinny though, they called me a Muselmann.
You know, a Muselmann means a step before dying.
They called them Muselmann.
My brother called me the Muselmann.
My feet were so swollen.
My hands were swollen.
My eyes were swollen.
My stomach was big.
And I had my wooden shoes, my [NON-ENGLISH].
And one of them, they killed a cow outside and they threw in a piece of skin with the hair from the cow to our barrack.
I would lay on the couch.
Somebody, one of the haftlinge, he was one of those prisoners threw in a large piece of skin instead of throwing it away.
They thought they might find some kind of necessity to use it.
He was sucking on it for hours.
And the guy next to me, he sucked on it.
And this place, they gave us a little bit more soup, with more ingredients inside than the previous ones.
And this was going on.
This was a little better, They didn't treat us as bad there, but we were treated much bad there from these other prisoners because they were very strong people.
There were a lot of German people there.
They were put in there for reasons that they committed to their own country, like murderers.
I don't know.
I was laying there with a blanket.
A blanket was my clothes.
Practically, a couple of weeks.
Without clothes, yeah.
And so what happened?
So I went ahead.
I made a hole in the blanket, and I put it through my head.
And I put a rope.
I put the bottom from the blanket, I tore a piece off, made like a girdle, which means like a rope, like a belt.
And I was going around.
That was my robe, and that's it.
Was your brother with you?
No.
My father was not with me nor my brother was with me.
I was there myself and everything I did there was myself.
I was worried about what had happened to them.
They must have died away because things were going so bad, indescribable.
Because I mentioned to you before, that's all-- I didn't look to live no more.
I looked only for one thing, to die.
I was praying to die, just praying.
Every night, I would say to myself, when?
When will it be?
Then we were taken out from that camp.
And they said we're going to evacuate the whole camp, evacuate.
And it took about five-- as I said to you that I couldn't walk.
We all went to a train.
They're going to evacuate us to Dachau and from Neckargerach to the train, must have been a couple of miles, a mile.
And we walked there, and I was dragging myself.
I'm sure somebody helped me walk because my feet were very swollen.
And we were protected the same way.
The first guy and this guy from the other side was responsible with the other three.
We had our hands locked in.
We went to a city called in Germany Osterburken.
It was near Neckargerach.
A little lady, well, a little girl-- went to a house there, apartments there, a little girl, threw down a piece of apple core, not an apple, a core.
And I caught it.
And I got beaten so hard from the guard.

He took the bat, hit me right over here, and he kicked me so bad until this day-- he hit me right here-- until this day, I have a tremendous big black spot here that now it became like a varicose vein and no blood going through.
This has been going on 50 years.
And we walked, and they dragged me.
All the other haftlinge because you have to-- I mean, the other prisoners.
They dragged me down to the train.
You know they say people, when it's misery, you get adjusted to misery, and you figure that's it.
And they took us to a train, and they put us on the train.
We heard the Americans were already about 3 or 4 miles, 5 miles away from us.
It was a matter of time.
And they put us on a train.
It was an open train, like they delivered coal.
It had practically 6 or 8 inches of snow.
It was a train open a flat.
And they put us in high, nobody could escape.
It was very high.
And first thing, they put me in there, and I sat down.
And the first thing I did, with my shoes, and I shoveled away the snow to have my feet there.
And I made sure that my blanket was under the snow.
And I sat there, put my head down, and I was just wondering what's going to be.
I didn't see nobody, and I didn't say nothing.
I didn't look at nobody.
No food.
And finally, the train was going back and forth, back and forth.
In that train, they had people from the whole world, that train.
I told you.
And it was going back and forth continuously.
Nobody said a word.
Everybody was quiet.
Supposedly, I heard after the war that they were going to take us back to Dachau to be killed.
Then somebody said that the last train, the last car had gasoline.
They were going to blow us up, which was true.
They were going to blow us up, and that's it.
Fortunately, there was a Frenchman that released the opening from that barrel.
He let all the gas out.
He knew what was going on.
Somebody told him that.
And the train went in partially to a tunnel, partially and stayed there because the airplanes were going and throwing bombs and everything.
We saw a little bit.
We saw a light.
My car was a little bit of light showing down, and the rest were in tunnel or something.
And I didn't know exactly-- I thought, like I mentioned before, to me it didn't matter anymore what it was going to be.
And kept very quiet.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It got very quiet.
And we sat there.
The Germans went away.
My car, the car was in, half way in a tunnel, there was half exposed of light, and we were maybe 3/4 length in the tunnel.
I know it was very dark.
I remember that today.
And somebody opened up the produce car.
And I heard they opened up, and they threw bread, the haftlinge.
The prisoners themselves opened up supply car.
They threw the bread.
They threw the margarine.
They threw over there, and it would have kept people to eat because there were no German no more.
And finally, I was so fortunate.
I caught a piece of margarine this big, long piece of margarine or fat or something.
I was so happy.
The same time another guy caught at the same time.
Give it to me.
Give it to me.
It's mine.
Give it to me.
It's mine.
It's mine.
He pulled it out of my hand.
He pulled it out.
I look at him.
He's my father, and he was in the same train.
I didn't even know about it.
He was in a different barrack.
My brother, he started to cry.
He said, how did you make out?
Where were you?
He said, I was in the same camp.
It was a different barrack in Neckargerach.
And he started to cry, and he kissed me and kissed me right on the train.
And I hurt, from the bottom I hurt.
Someone screamed.
The opened up the gate from the train.
I heard some screaming, Mendel, Heim Schteinberg.
Heim Schteinberg.
Mendel.
Everybody was calling these names.
Finally, my brother was walking around.
He was in a different camp.
They combined all these cars, that tunnel, they all belonged to Dachau.
So they organized one train where all these people from all these camps.
Finally, my brother, he saw me, oh, start crying.
He wore a German uniform because he was naked.
And he took off a German uniform from a German guy who was killed there.
When the Germans escaped, the first thing they did, took of the uniform, the SS, and they throw it away.
They went away.
They left with their underwear.
And we stayed there-- I don't know exactly in that tunnel, three of four days.
The war was going on terribly.
We couldn't go out.
People getting shot.
Anybody who went out of the tunnel get shot because the Germans and the Americans were fighting very bad.
The French people, there was a little puddle of water there.
There was snails.
They showed us how to cook snails.
They were eating snails, and they showed us how to do it.
And they were eating that.
And they had flowers there.
They had little flowers there with large stems, with milk inside.
They showed us how to suck this out.
This got nutrition.
You can eat that.
My brother, he was wounded.
In the bombs, he was wounded.
So I don't know what happened to him.
After he saw us and he embraced us, he took off.
I think he managed to go to the camp to the Americans and tell him that the camp, a big train of Jews in a tunnel, please help them.
Definitely the Jews, a lot of people, they are tunneled there.
They are prisoners of concentration camps.
My brother told me yesterday, he did it, which I know is true because he doesn't tell fibs.
And that's what happened.
He said I was taken.
I was taken out to an ambulance, and before I walked out, I saw a German SS man hanging.
And I took off his shoes.
Not me.
Asked to put his shoes on.
They put it on me.
I couldn't do that no more.
My clothes?
I was taken to a hospital, right in the small city of Osterburken.
We went to a city called Meuspath, right next to each other.
And they transferred a little small hotel for a hospital.
I went in there.
And this is what I was given.
Oh, yeah.
The cap I wore all through the concentration camps.
This is my cap.
I must have got it in Radom.
Yeah, but my brother, he twice tried to take possession of it.
My big head?
No.
But I got my number here.
My name is engraved here.
It's written up here, and my number is here.
So shall I put it on?
Yeah.
It's funny what Jewish people have to go through.
Right?
In life.
Just because we're Jews.
In the hospital, I went to the hospital.
A person must have broke into Meuspath, and they found out I had a complete lung infected with water.
It was called pleurisy.
And a Italian doctor came there, also a prisoner of war, a prisoner.
And he put in a needle between my lungs.
It was the most awful pain I ever had in my life.
He pulled out the water.
And then after that hospital, I lived about seven or eight weeks, I stayed there.
And that's how they got me back to normal.
In that hospital, they gave me a new uniform, striped.
They told me if you want to have it as a memory of the concentration camp, have this uniform.
My youngest one?
They were taken away.
They were killed.
Oh.
My brother went away.
The UNRRA took over, and they took him, I think, to a place called Schloss Langenzell, near Germany.
My father went there too, I think.
He went to an operation because he had a big rupture from being kicked.
So he went to an operation.
And I remember my brother, a Jewish soldier gave him $1, my brother.
So he came, and he gave it to the nurse.
He said this is $1.
It was just given.
Please, take the dollar and help my brother.
He gave her that dollar.
I went in the hospital.
We were finally reunited when they took me to a city called Stuttgart.
And I felt much better.
And I was in the hospital three month.
I got my weight back and all.
My swelling went away, my-- came back to normal conditions, and I began to live.
People were not used to catch Germans, and I never thought of it.
I never thought about that.
Beating Germans, I never wanted to do that.
I stayed in Stuttgart for about May or June till about one year.
It was an UNRRA camp sponsored by the Joint Distribution Committee.
I went to school there.
I went to a school called [NON-ENGLISH].
No.
No.
No.
No.
No.
We all went together and stayed in the same house, stayed in the same apartment.
My father opened a little place of work.
He worked for the ex prisoners, friends of ours.
He made them suits because material was easy to get.
It was confiscated from the Germans.
My brother, I can't recall.
He must have gone to school because he had little schooling, so he must have gone to school, something.
